construct_description: The ability to formulate clear claims, select and evaluate evidence from multiple sources, address counterarguments, and communicate a well-reasoned position in writing.

dimensions.name: Claim Clarity
dimensions.description: States a clear, specific position that directly addresses the prompt

dimensions.name: Evidence Use
dimensions.description: Selects relevant evidence from provided sources and integrates it effectively

dimensions.name: Source Evaluation
dimensions.description: Distinguishes stronger from weaker sources and addresses credibility

dimensions.name: Counterargument
dimensions.description: Acknowledges opposing viewpoints and responds substantively

dimensions.name: Writing Quality
dimensions.description: Organized, clear, professional tone appropriate for the audience

scenario.title: The AI Tutoring Decision
scenario.prompt: The Riverside Unified School District is considering a $2.4M contract to adopt MathMentor AI, an AI-powered tutoring platform, across all middle schools. As a policy analyst, write a position memo to the school board recommending whether to adopt, modify, or reject the proposal. Support your position with evidence from the provided sources.

sources.title: AI Tutoring Systems in K-12 Mathematics: A Meta-Analysis
sources.author: Chen, Rodriguez & Park
sources.content_html:
This meta-analysis examined 47 randomized controlled trials of AI tutoring systems in K-12 mathematics classrooms (N = 31,400 students) published between 2019 and 2024.

Key findings:

  • Students using AI tutoring showed a mean effect size of d = 0.38 on standardized math assessments compared to control groups — a moderate, statistically significant improvement.
  • Effects were strongest for students performing in the 25th–50th percentile at baseline (d = 0.52), suggesting AI tutoring may be most effective for struggling learners.
  • Effects diminished after 18 months of use (d = 0.18), raising questions about long-term sustainability.
  • Implementation quality was the strongest moderator: schools with dedicated training for teachers saw nearly double the effect size of those without.
  • No significant differences were found between specific AI platforms when controlling for implementation quality.

Limitations: Most studies were 6–12 months in duration. Only 8 of 47 studies examined effects beyond one academic year. Publication bias may inflate reported effect sizes.

sources.title: MathMentor AI: The App That's Dividing Educators
sources.author: Sarah Kowalski
sources.content_html:
MathMentor AI has been called "revolutionary" by its CEO, Dylan Voss, who claims the platform "delivers the equivalent of a personal tutor for every student at a fraction of the cost."

The company points to its pilot program in the Austin Independent School District, where MathMentor schools saw a 23% increase in state math proficiency rates over two years. "The data speaks for itself," Voss said at the National EdTech Conference last month.

But critics note the Austin pilot was not a controlled experiment. Dr. Maria Santos, an education researcher at UT Austin, said: "The MathMentor schools also received additional funding, new textbooks, and smaller class sizes as part of a broader initiative. Attributing the gains solely to the AI platform is misleading."

Teacher reactions are mixed. "My students love it — engagement is through the roof," said 7th-grade teacher James Wu. But veteran math teacher Linda Okafor pushed back: "They're clicking through gamified exercises, not developing mathematical thinking. When I give them a novel problem, they're just as lost as before."

MathMentor's $2.4M price tag for a mid-size district includes licensing, hardware upgrades, and first-year support — but not the ongoing annual renewal of $600K, a detail some board members have flagged.

sources.title: Open Letter from the Riverside Parent-Teacher Alliance
sources.author: Riverside PTA Board
sources.content_html:
Dear Members of the School Board,

As parents and caregivers of Riverside students, we write with serious concerns about the proposed MathMentor AI contract.

Our concerns:

  • Screen time: Our children already spend 4-6 hours daily on screens. Adding AI tutoring sessions means more time staring at devices instead of engaging with teachers and peers.
  • Data privacy: MathMentor collects detailed behavioral data on every student interaction. Their privacy policy allows sharing aggregated data with "research partners." We do not believe sufficient safeguards are in place.
  • Teacher replacement: We've heard rumors that the district plans to increase class sizes once MathMentor is in place. Our children need human teachers, not algorithms.
  • Cost: $2.4 million could fund 30 additional teaching positions. We believe investing in people, not software, is the better path.

We urge the board to reject this proposal and instead invest in hiring and retaining qualified math teachers.

Respectfully,
The Riverside Parent-Teacher Alliance
(Representing 847 families)

sources.title: MathMentor AI: 5-Year Cost-Benefit Projection
sources.author: Riverside USD Office of Finance
sources.content_html:
ItemYear 1Years 2-5 (annual)5-Year Total
Platform licensing$1,200,000$600,000$3,600,000
Hardware upgrades$480,000$0$480,000
Teacher training$320,000$80,000$640,000
IT support (0.5 FTE)$45,000$45,000$225,000
Total$2,045,000$725,000$4,945,000

Comparison scenario — hiring 15 new math teachers:

ItemYear 1Years 2-5 (annual)5-Year Total
Salaries (avg $62K)$930,000$958,000*$4,804,000
Benefits (32%)$297,600$306,500$1,523,600
Onboarding & PD$120,000$30,000$240,000
Total$1,347,600$1,294,500$6,567,600

* Assumes 3% annual salary increase

Notes: The MathMentor contract does not include a performance guarantee. The district may terminate after Year 1 with a $180,000 early termination fee. Teacher hiring projections assume current labor market conditions; recruitment in math is historically challenging (average time-to-fill: 4.2 months).

sources.title: MathMentor AI: Riverside Pilot Usage Report (Q1 2026)
sources.author: MathMentor Analytics Team
sources.content_html:
Pilot deployment across 3 Riverside middle schools (1,240 students, 14 classrooms).

MetricValue
Average sessions per student per week3.2
Average session duration18 minutes
Concept mastery rate (platform-defined)71%
Student satisfaction (survey, n=890)4.1 / 5.0
Teacher satisfaction (survey, n=14)3.4 / 5.0
Students completing all assigned modules58%
Help-desk tickets submitted127

Student performance (pilot schools vs. district average):

AssessmentPilot SchoolsDistrict AvgDifference
Q1 Benchmark (% proficient)64%59%+5 pts
Q1 Benchmark (avg score)72.368.1+4.2 pts

Caveats: Pilot schools were selected by the vendor based on "readiness criteria." Comparison is not randomized. Platform-defined "concept mastery" uses a proprietary algorithm and may not align with state standards. Only 58% of students completed all assigned modules, which may skew results toward more engaged students.

coaching_tips: Strong memos state a clear position in the opening sentence — don't leave the reader guessing.
Reference at least 3 sources and explain why you trust some more than others.
Identify which sources are vendor-provided vs. independent — this matters for credibility.
The best counterarguments don't just list objections — they respond to them with evidence.
Budget comparisons become more persuasive when you address the 5-year total, not just Year 1.
Note the caveats in the usage data: pilot schools were vendor-selected and completion was only 58%.
